abstract:
Rhythm resides in the mind: Reflections on rhythm in natural language

This article explores contemporary theories regarding the nature of rhythm in natural languages. Traditionally, languages are categorized into three rhythmic types based on the perception of stress, syllable, or mora. In stress-timed languages, accented syllables are thought to occur at regular intervals; conversely, in syllable-timed and mora-timed languages, it is the syllables or moras that appear to follow an equal temporal distribution. Although extensive phonetic research has failed to provide empirical evidence for physical isochrony in the speech stream, a wealth of data suggests that rhythm remains a fundamental component of language processing. This paper argues that phonetic models treating rhythm as a mere secondary phenomenon are insufficient for describing diverse linguistic systems. Ultimately, we demonstrate that the intuitive concept of rhythm is an essential element that must be integrated into formal phonological analysis.
